retrieved_docs.title: Suicide: Assessment and Management -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Electronic health records screening for suicidality using artificial intelligence, internet-based screening, and smartphone passive monitoring to identify high-risk patients is understudied, but it also raises privacy concerns. Machine learning has been applied to the prediction of suicidal behavior and large electronic health records data sets, but has a low positive predictive value due to the low base rate of suicidal behavior in the population. [5] [63] Studies assessing neuroinflammation using the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio, the platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio, and the monocyte-to-lymphocyte ratio as indicators of systemic inflammation related to suicidality highlight the need for further research in this area. [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Suicide: Assessment and Management -- Treatment / Management -- Disposition and the Level of Care
retrieved_docs.content: Inpatient psychiatric hospitalization is the standard of care for patients with high suicide risk. However, the relationship between hospitalization and future suicidal behaviors is poorly understood. A recent study on patients seen at the Veterans Health Administration used machine learning on observational data, and the results found that hospitalization was associated with reduced suicide attempt risk only among individuals who had attempted suicide in the prior day, underscoring the importance of individualized treatment approaches. The study's results also noted that suicide risk is only one of many reasons for a patient to be hospitalized for psychiatric treatment. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Allen Cognitive Level -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Claudia K Allen and her colleagues formulated the Cognitive Disabilities Model (CDM), which stemmed from their observations of adult patients with persistent mental disorders such as schizophrenia and major depressive disorder. According to the CDM, most of these patients will experience diminishing abilities to perform activities of daily living (ADLs) and to function within their communities due to their mental illness. This cognitive impairment may be either temporary or permanent. Six cognitive levels, termed Allen Cognitive Levels, were derived from the CDM. The Allen Cognitive Level Screen (ACLS) was published in 1985, providing a tool to help quickly stratify the observed cognitive disabilities. The ACLS allowed assessment of patients’ cognitive functioning via observation of sensorimotor actions. The ACLS comprises a series of increasingly complex leather lacing stitches, and a numerical score ranging from 3.0 to 5.8 derives from how accurately the test subject can replicate the therapist’s stitches. A score of 5.8 predicts that the patient will be able to function well on his or her own, but a score of less than 5.8 indicates that the patient may benefit from additional assistance. This score allows the therapist to estimate the patient’s capability in learning, problem-solving and following directions. While modifications to the ACLS have been made since its original publication, it remains one of the most widely utilized tools for occupational therapists to help assess their patients. Because of the ACLS’s limited scoring range, patients at a cognitive level of less than 3 are unable to be test subjects for the screening tool. The test is also unable to accurately assess the patient’s ability to plan for the future, so its maximum score is capped at 5.8. [1] [2] [3] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Outcome Assessment and Information Set -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Brown et al studied the detection of depression among HHC patients, and the primary outcome of the study suggested that depression is underdiagnosed among the elderly at HHC. [5] On January 1, 2008, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services implemented the Home Health Resource Group (HHRG) scoring to overcome this problem. The clinician completes the HHRG scoring and assesses the severity, utilization of services, and functional status of the patients. [3] [6] With this new model, there were 153 possible HHRG, compared with the 80 different possibilities before. Screening for depressive symptoms is now part of the criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: Posttraumatic Stress Disorder -- Differential Diagnosis -- Major Depressive Disorder
retrieved_docs.content: Affective changes are common in PTSD, and major depressive disorder (MDD) can be a comorbid condition with PTSD. Diagnosis of MDD requires at least 1 major depressive episode, which is persistent decreased mood for at least 2 weeks. [68]

retrieved_docs.title: Major Depressive Disorder -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Severe early stress can result in drastic alterations in neuroendocrine and behavioral responses, which can cause structural changes in the cerebral cortex, leading to severe depression later in life. Structural and functional brain imaging of depressed individuals has shown increased hyperintensities in the subcortical regions, and reduced anterior brain metabolism on the left side, respectively. Family, adoption, and twin studies have indicated the role of genes in the susceptibility of depression. Genetic studies show a very high concordance rate for twins to have MDD, particularly monozygotic twins. [4] Life events and personality traits have shown to play an important role, as well. The learned helplessness theory has associated the occurrence of depression with the experience of uncontrollable events. Per cognitive theory, depression occurs as a result of cognitive distortions in persons who are susceptible to depression.

retrieved_docs.title: Body Image Distortion (Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis -- Major Depressive Disorder
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with major depressive disorder experience alteration in posture and dissatisfaction with body image during episodes of depression.

retrieved_docs.title: Depressive Cognitive Disorders  -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Assess cognitive domains, including memory, executive function, attention, and processing speed, using validated clinical tools in patients with depressive symptoms. Compare depressive cognitive disorders with neurocognitive disorders to distinguish overlapping features and refine diagnostic accuracy. Differentiate reversible depressive cognitive disorders from progressive neurodegenerative conditions by integrating clinical evaluation, psychiatric history, and structured assessments. Coordinate with the interprofessional team to assess, diagnose, and treat patients with depressive cognitive disorder to improve patient outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Myers-Briggs Type Indicator -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Although the MBTI was not originally designed for clinical use, it has been applied to certain patient populations. In psychology and psychiatry, the MBTI may provide insights into specific groups, such as those experiencing suicidality and unipolar depression. Research has shown that these populations tend to exhibit greater tendencies toward introversion in energy and perceiving in orientation than the general population. Researchers suggest that, with further confirmatory studies, these correlations could help identify vulnerability in patients with affective disorders. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Depressive Cognitive Disorders  -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with neurodegenerative dementia exhibit a considerably faster rate of forgetting as compared to depressed and normal people, who forget data at a similar rate. Depressed individuals with memory impairment also tend to show less random variation in their responses. A clear-cut ‘unified theory’ for cognitive impairment in depression has not been formulated, but research points to an encoding issue seen in these patients. Depressive disorders are associated with automatic negative thoughts and rumination, which encroach on conscious thought and affect the encoding of memories. [17] Additionally, in depression, a decrease in motivation and concentration negatively impacts the effort applied to initial learning. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2287
retrieved_docs.content: Major depressive disorder with a recurrent episode, major depressive disorder with a single episode, major depressive disorder with a single episode in full remission, atypical depression (اكتئاب أوبِيّ), and mood lability (التقلقل الوجداني) are all classified as members of the schizophrenia and psychotic disorders SMQ (الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)). The SMQ itself is categorized under the broader construct of blunted affect (عاطفة متقلصة). This hierarchical organization indicates that these depressive conditions are considered within the spectrum of schizophrenia‑related and psychotic disorders, and that the entire group is associated with a reduction in emotional expressivity. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians appreciate the overlap between mood disturbances and psychotic features, and underscores the importance of assessing affective flattening when evaluating patients with these diagnoses.

retrieved_docs.content: hub that connects dementia, post‑concussive states, depressive mood, psychotic features, and cholinergic antagonism within a unified nosological framework, supporting comprehensive assessment and targeted management of patients whose psychiatric symptoms arise from or are intertwined with underlying medical or neurological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 254
retrieved_docs.content: integrating various organic‑based mood and transient disturbances that do not fit more specific categories. This structured framework aids clinicians in accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 962
retrieved_docs.content: The category “disorders usually diagnosed in infancy” has a broader relationship with ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة (unspecified childhood psychosis, current or active). This specific psychotic condition is a member of the larger classification الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) (psychosis and psychotic disorders). Within the الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) group, several mood‑related diagnoses are also listed as members: اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة (major depressive disorder, single episode), اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), اكتئاب أَوبِيّ (atypical depression), and the symptom التقلقل الوجداني (mood lability). Moreover, the entire الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) category is itself a member of عاطفة متقلصة (blunted affect). This network of relationships illustrates the overlap between early‑onset psychotic conditions, broader psychotic disorder classifications, depressive mood disorders, and affective symptoms such as blunted affect, emphasizing the need for integrated assessment and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1886
retrieved_docs.content: Behavioral/psychiatric manifestations are classified as a subcategory of the broader entity الهَذَيان, indicating that these mental and behavioral signs are considered part of the larger domain represented by الهَذَيان. This hierarchical relationship helps organize clinical features, allowing clinicians to recognize that behavioral and psychiatric presentations fall within the overarching concept of الهَذَيان and to consider them in the context of that broader classification when assessing and managing patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2766
retrieved_docs.content: Psychological disorder (اضطراب نفسي) is a broad category of mental health conditions that encompasses more specific disorders such as depression (اكتئاب) and separation‑anxiety disorder (اضطراب قلق الانفصال), each of which is a narrower subtype within the overall class of psychological disorders. Both the general category of psychological disorder and its child conditions—depression and separation‑anxiety disorder—are identified as targets for prevention and control measures, indicating that strategies aimed at preventing onset, reducing severity, and managing symptoms are applicable across the spectrum of these mental health conditions. This hierarchical relationship underscores the importance of applying preventive and control interventions at both the overarching disorder level and the individual disorder level to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 252
retrieved_docs.content: cognitive and sleep disturbances in non‑infectious cerebral dysfunction. Overall, the community’s model depicts a hierarchical organization in which a general mental‑symptom framework branches into specific depressive subtypes, which are interrelated with psychotic, encephalitic, delirious, motor, sleep‑wake, and cognitive symptom clusters, emphasizing the interconnected nature of affective, psychotic, inflammatory, and behavioral processes for clinical assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2294
retrieved_docs.content: Transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type is closely related to, and may be synonymous with, mood disorders, indicating that this transient organic psychotic condition is considered within the spectrum of mood disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 435
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)” is presented as a broad grouping that encompasses a variety of psychotic and affective conditions. Within this group are several specific entities: “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” (unspecified childhood psychosis, current or active), “هوس شَبَقِيّ” (schizotypal mania), and “عاطفة متقلصة” (blunted affect). In addition to primary psychotic disorders, the SMQ grouping also includes mood‑related diagnoses such as “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة” (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة” (major depressive disorder, single episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة” (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), “اكتئاب أَوبِيّ” (atypical depression), and “التقلقل الوجداني” (affective lability). This reflects an integrated view in which depressive disorders and affective symptoms are considered part of the broader psychosis spectrum, especially when they may present with psychotic features. The entity “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” is also linked to the more general category “disorders usually diagnosed in infancy,” indicating that this form of childhood psychosis is typically identified early in life. Furthermore, “هوس شَبَقِيّ” is related to “ذُهان عضوي” (organic psychosis) through a broader‑relationship link, suggesting that schizotypal mania can be viewed as a subtype or manifestation of an organic psychotic process. Overall, the SMQ framework integrates early‑onset psychotic conditions, organic psychoses, specific manic presentations, and a range of depressive and affective disorders, highlighting the clinical overlap among psychotic and mood disturbances and underscoring the importance of considering these interrelationships in diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2898
retrieved_docs.content: Mental disorder (اضطراب نفسي) is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses more specific conditions such as depression (اكتئاب) and separation anxiety disorder (اضطراب قلق الانفصال). Separation anxiety disorder is identified as a narrower form within the overall class of mental disorders, while depression is listed as a child concept of mental disorder, indicating its status as a distinct subtype. Epidemiology is recognized as an appropriate qualifier for mental disorder, depression, and separation anxiety disorder, underscoring the relevance of epidemiological research in describing the prevalence, distribution, and risk factors of these psychiatric conditions. This hierarchical organization and the emphasis on epidemiological data together support a comprehensive understanding of how these disorders are related and inform patient care and public‑health strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2292
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition, also described as anxiety disorder due to a known physiological condition, is a distinct psychiatric diagnosis that requires the presence of specific diagnostic criteria. This disorder is characterized by anxiety symptoms that are directly attributable to an underlying medical or physiological condition, and clinicians must identify the causal medical condition and apply the established criteria to confirm the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2574
retrieved_docs.content: Emotional blunting (تبلد المشاعر) is identified as a clinical manifestation of specific brain atrophy (ضمور المخ المحدد). This relationship indicates that patients who exhibit localized cerebral atrophy may present with reduced emotional responsiveness, and recognizing emotional blunting as a sign of such atrophy can aid clinicians in diagnosing underlying neurological conditions where affective changes are a prominent feature.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 534
retrieved_docs.content: Other specified transient mental disorders due to conditions classified elsewhere are positioned within both the mood disorders and anxiety disorders categories, reflecting that this diagnostic group exhibits features common to each of these major psychiatric domains. In a similar vein, the Transient organic psychotic condition, depressive type is closely linked to mood disorders and may be regarded as essentially synonymous with them, underscoring its classification as a mood‑related disorder. Together, these relationships highlight the overlapping nature of certain transient mental health conditions across traditional mood and anxiety disorder boundaries, which is important for accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2561
retrieved_docs.content: Other and unspecified mental conditions and Other mental conditions are both used as classification categories for the disorder اضطراب المعارض المتحدي. In addition, the category Other mental conditions is itself classified as the disorder اضطراب الاجترال, indicating that Other mental conditions may be considered a subset or related concept within اضطراب الاجترال. This hierarchical arrangement shows how broader mental health categories are employed to group specific disorders such as اضطراب المعارض المتحدي and how certain categories can also be linked to particular diagnoses like اضطراب الاجترال, facilitating organized diagnostic coding and clinical reference.
